perturbation.original_text: 1.16 "Drilling Costs" means the actual costs and expenses incurred in drilling a well subject to this Agreement beginning with the initiation of preliminary site preparation activities through and including logging, testing and evaluating the well prior to recommending whether to attempt a Completion. Drilling Costs shall include, but shall not be limited to, the costs and expenses associated with permitting, preparing the site, drilling to the Objective Zone, and evaluating any Zone(s) in such well to which a Completion may be attempted, as well as any unplanned or unforeseen events such as fire, explosion, or loss of well control. Drilling Costs shall also include brokerage, abstracting, and reasonable attorney fees related to the preparation of drilling title opinions for such well. Drilling Costs shall not include the cost to plug and abandon any well, including a dry hole, and shall not include any Completion Costs.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.16 "Drilling Costs" means the costs associated with drilling a well subject to this Agreement. Drilling Costs shall include only the amounts paid to the drilling company for the physical drilling of the well. All other costs, including, but not limited to, the costs and expenses associated with permitting, preparing the site, drilling to the Objective Zone, and evaluating any Zone(s) in such well to which a Completion may be attempted, as well as any unplanned or unforeseen events such as fire, explosion, or loss of well control are excluded. Drilling Costs shall not include brokerage, abstracting, reasonable attorney fees related to the preparation of drilling title opinions, the cost to plug and abandon any well, including a dry hole, and shall not include any Completion Costs.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Drilling Costs" was modified to exclude expenses typically included under oil and gas accounting practices, creating a conflict. This introduces ambiguity regarding cost allocation, particularly concerning expenses such as permitting, site preparation, and legal fees which are usually considered part of drilling costs.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Louisiana Administrative Code Title 43, Part XIX, §109.B.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Louisiana Administrative Code Title 43, Part XIX, §109.B, which implicitly recognizes that drilling costs encompass more than just payments to the drilling company. The rule pertains to ownership of production and requires allocation factors, which depend on accurate accounting of all costs associated with drilling and completing a well, including expenses beyond mere payments to the drilling contractor.
perturbation.justification: The modification to the definition of "Drilling Costs" creates a contradiction because it deviates from standard oil and gas accounting practices, making the contract unenforceable due to misaligned terminology, which falls under the definition of "Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction". The Perturbation Explanation states that the modification excludes expenses typically included under oil and gas accounting practices, specifically mentioning permitting, site preparation, and legal fees. The Law Explanation states that the rule pertains to ownership of production and requires allocation factors, which depend on accurate accounting of all costs associated with drilling, including expenses beyond mere payments to the drilling contractor. This directly supports the claim that limiting "Drilling Costs" solely to payments to the drilling company contradicts the implicit recognition of broader costs under the law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 The Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Well shall be under the exclusive control of BP and the operation thereof shall be conducted in a prudent and workmanlike manner. BP shall conduct all its activities under this Agreement as a reasonable prudent operator, in a good and workmanlike manner, with due diligence and dispatch, in accordance with good oilfield practice, and in compliance with applicable law and regulation, but in no event shall BP have any liability to Company for losses sustained or liabilities or obligations incurred except such as may result from BP's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 The Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Well shall be under the exclusive control of BP and the operation thereof shall be conducted with minimal oversight. BP shall conduct its activities under this Agreement with an expectation of good faith, but shall only be liable to Company for losses sustained or liabilities or obligations incurred if BP intentionally disregards safety regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The standard of care required of BP was significantly lowered from a "prudent and workmanlike manner" to "minimal oversight" and limiting liability to only instances of intentionally disregarding safety regulations. This substantially weakens the protection afforded to True North Energy Corp. and lowers the liability requirements for BP.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts Louisiana Civil Code Article 2710, which implies a duty of care that aligns more closely with a 'prudent administrator' or 'reasonable operator' standard. By lowering the standard of care and limiting liability to intentional disregard of safety, the modified text allows for a level of negligence that would be unacceptable under Louisiana law governing contractual obligations.

perturbation.justification: The modification significantly lowers the standard of care required of BP. The original text stipulates that BP's operations be conducted in a "prudent and workmanlike manner," while the changed text only requires "minimal oversight" and limits liability to instances of "intentionally disregarding safety regulations." This directly contradicts the duty of care implied by Louisiana Civil Code Article 2710, according to the Law Explanation, which aligns with a 'prudent administrator' or 'reasonable operator' standard. The revised language allows for a level of negligence unacceptable under Louisiana law, which governs contractual obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 5.6 Unless Company elects by thirty (30) days' prior written notice to BP either to take in kind or to separately dispose of its share of oil, gas and other hydrocarbons, BP shall in good faith, to the extent it can do so, cause Company's share of production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Wells to be marketed and sold to either a Third Party or to an Affiliate of BP in a commercially reasonable manner, which terms shall not be less than on the same terms and conditions as BP's share of production from such wells are sold. It is recognized by the Parties that BP, or its predecessor, has provided at its cost or made arrangements with Third Parties to provide certain facilities beyond the wellhead (the "Plants") needed for producing, storing, separating, gathering, treating, processing and delivering production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Well. It is agreed that BP will continue to make the Plants (as they or any contractual arrangements related thereto may be modified, changed or upgraded) proportionately available to handle BP, Company and Third Party production from the Contract Area. It is understood that a proportionate share of the cost of maintaining and operating the Plants, including depreciation or rental in lieu of depreciation and actual Third Party costs, whether on a cash fee basis or on a retained volume basis, will be allocated to the Parties on a "throughput" basis (being that portion of such costs relating to the production volumes from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well, as each may bear to the total production volumes handled by the Plants, including any Third Party or BP volumes not produced from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well). Nothing herein shall be construed to impart, transfer or convey any ownership interest in the Plants to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.6 Unless Company elects by thirty (30) days' prior written notice to BP either to take in kind or to separately dispose of its share of oil, gas and other hydrocarbons, BP will market and sell Company's share of production as BP sees fit, which may be to a Third Party or to an Affiliate of BP, or in any other way BP sees fit. BP does not need to offer similar prices or terms as their own share of production. BP may make changes to its facilities at its discretion, and may make the Plants available in any proportion to Company. Company agrees to bear its share of the cost regardless of how production volumes are handled by the Plant.
perturbation.explanation: This revision gives BP complete and unchecked discretion in marketing and selling True North Energy Corp.'s share of production, removing the requirement for good faith and commercially reasonable terms. It also gives BP more leeway to change the way it uses the facilities at their discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change potentially violates Louisiana Civil Code Article 1983, which states that contracts must be performed in good faith. By granting BP unfettered discretion in marketing True North Energy Corp.'s production and allocating plant costs, the modified text enables BP to act in a manner that is not in good faith, such as selling True North Energy Corp.'s share at significantly lower prices than its own or disproportionately allocating plant costs. The original text mandates a good faith obligation on BP.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Louisiana Administrative Code | State Regulations | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Louisiana Administrative Code Title 1 - ADMINISTRATIVE LAW Title 4 - ADMINISTRATION Title 7 - AGRICULTURE AND ANIMALS Title 10 - FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS, CONSUMER CREDIT, INVESTMENT SECURITIES AND UCC Title 13 - ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT Title 16 - COMMUNITY AFFAIRS Title 17 - CONSTRUCTION Title 19 - CORPORATION AND BUSINESS Title 22 - CORRECTIONS, CRIMINAL JUSTICE AND LAW ENFORCEMENT Title 25 - CULTURAL RESOURCES Title 28 - EDUCATION Title 31 - ELECTIONS Title 32 - EMPLOYEE BENEFITS Title 33 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY Title 34 - GOVERNMENT CONTRACTS, PROCUREMENT AND PROPERTY CONTROL Title 35 - HORSE RACING Title 37 - INSURANCE Title 40 - LABOR AND EMPLOYMENT Title 41 - MILITARY FORCES OF THE STATE Title 42 - LOUISIANA GAMING Title 43 - NATURAL RESOURCES Title 46 - PROFESSIONAL AND OCCUPATIONAL STANDARDS Title 48 - PUBLIC HEALTH-GENERAL Title 49 - PUBLIC HEALTH-FOOD, DRUGS, AND COSMETICS Title 50 - PUBLIC HEALTH-MEDICAL ASSISTANCE Title 51 - PUBLIC HEALTH-SANITARY CODE Title 52 - ETHICS Title 55 - PUBLIC SAFETY Title 56 - PUBLIC WORKS Title 58 - RETIREMENT Title 61 - REVENUE AND TAXATION Title 67 - SOCIAL SERVICES Title 69 - TAX APPEALS (REVENUE DEPARTMENT AND LOCAL SALES TAX DISPUTES) Title 70 - TRANSPORTATION AND DEVELOPMENT Title 71 - TREASURY-PUBLIC FUNDS Title 72 - UNIFORM LOCAL SALES TAX Title 73 - WEIGHTS, MEASURES AND STANDARDS Title 76 - WILDLIFE AND FISHERIES State Regulations Toolbox about
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of good faith in contract performance, as suggested by the Law Explanation, which references Louisiana Civil Code Article 1983. The original text required BP to act in good faith and in a commercially reasonable manner when marketing True North Energy Corp.'s share of production. The revised text removes these requirements, granting BP unfettered discretion and potentially allowing them to act unfairly. The original agreement to make the plants proportionately available is also undermined by the changed text allowing BP to allocate the Plants in any proportion. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly state Article 1983, they do show the laws of Louisiana, giving credence to the Law Explanation and its relevance to the original agreement.
